original_abstract:
In contrast to the customized synthetic strategies typically used to construct specific natural products, biosynthetic pathways often leverage the divergent cyclization of a common polyunsaturated intermediate to generate structural variety. Taking inspiration from these natural processes, we report a flexible, divergent synthetic platform for the efficient assembly of monoterpene indole alkaloids. Central to this approach is the development of a pluripotent dihydropyridine-vinylindole intermediate. By modulating the reactivity of this common precursor through specific functional group manipulations and reaction conditions, we achieved systematic access to five distinct skeletal frameworks—including the aspidosperma, iboga, andranginine, and ngouniensine types, as well as a non-natural variant—in only 6 to 9 steps from tryptamine.

Key to our methodology is a copper(I)-catalyzed 6-endo-dig cyclization that forms the sensitive dihydropyridine core under mild conditions. We demonstrate the utility of this strategy through three distinct modes of [4+2] cyclizations and two redox-mediated annulations. The practical efficiency and versatility of the assembly line were further validated by the total syntheses of (±)-vincadifformine, (±)-andranginine, and (−)-catharanthine, with the latter representing the shortest enantiocontrolled synthesis reported to date. This work establishes a programmable framework for the diversification of complex architectures, offering a blueprint for emulating biogenetic divergence in the laboratory.
humanized_abstract:
While tailored synthetic methods are generally required to build specific natural products, in many cases the diversity of natural products is produced through the divergent cyclization of a common precursor, in this case a common polyunsaturated precursor. We herein demonstrate a general and versatile approach for the synthesis of monoterpene indole alkaloids (MIAs) through divergent cyclization of a common dihydropyridine-vinylindole precursor. This precursor was produced in a few steps from tryptamine using standard manipulations and conditions to switch on and off its reactivity. Through the use of this unique precursor we were able to prepare in 6-9 steps and in good yields five different types of MIA skeletons including aspidosperma, iboga, andranginine and ngouniensine structures as well as a new artificial type.

We report a modular and flexible “assembly line” approach based on the key copper(I)-catalyzed 6-endo-dig cyclization of a diene to form the dihydropyridine core of the indole alkaloids. Within this assembly line, we have demonstrated three different modes of [4+2] cyclizations and two redox-mediated annulations. To showcase the efficiency and versatility of the strategy, we have used it for the total syntheses of (±)-vincadifformine and (±)-andranginine as well as (−)-catharanthine, the last one being the shortest enantiocontrolled synthesis reported to date. Our results provide a programmable, efficient, and flexible diversification method for complex structures and give us a tool to emulate biogenetic divergence in the laboratory.

document.output:

Through the use of this unique precursor we were able to prepare in 6-9 steps and in good yields five different types of MIA skeletons including aspidosperma, iboga, andranginine and ngouniensine structures as well as a new artificial type.

We report a modular and flexible “assembly line” approach based on the key copper(I)-catalyzed 6-endo-dig cyclization of a diene to form the dihydropyridine core of the indole alkaloids. document.input:
